label: Worker Becomes a Reporting Person Upon Submitting a Report
warning: Once a worker submits a report through any channel, they become a protected 'reporting person' under the Directive. The obligated entity is immediately subject to anti-retaliation obligations, confidentiality requirements, and a reversed burden of proof in any subsequent proceedings. Seek legal advice before taking any adverse employment action that could be linked to the report.

label: Private Entity Crossing the 50-Worker Threshold Becomes an Obligated Entity
warning: With 50 or more workers, this entity is now required to establish an internal reporting channel and implement all associated obligations (designation, confidentiality, anti-retaliation, GDPR). Entities between 50–249 workers may share a reporting channel with other SMEs under Article 8(3). Verify compliance status and establish the channel without undue delay.

label: Municipality Crossing 10,000-Inhabitant Threshold Becomes an Obligated Entity
warning: A municipality reaching 10,000 inhabitants loses its Article 8(9) exemption and must establish an internal reporting channel. All public-sector obligations under the Directive then apply. Verify the current population figure against the latest official census and act accordingly.

label: Job Applicant Who Reports During Recruitment Becomes a Reporting Person
warning: A job applicant who reports a breach encountered during the recruitment process is entitled to full protection as a reporting person, even without an employment contract. Any rejection or adverse treatment that could be linked to the report may constitute prohibited retaliation. Seek legal advice before making any recruitment decision involving this applicant.

label: Former Worker Retains Reporting Person Protections for Post-Employment Reports
warning: A former worker who reports a breach acquired during their employment retains full reporting-person protections. The former employer cannot take retaliatory action (e.g. negative references, legal proceedings) linked to the report. The reversed burden of proof applies in any retaliation proceedings.

label: Reporting Person Who Knowingly Makes a False Report Loses All Protections
warning: A reporting person who knowingly reported false information is not entitled to protection under the Directive and may be subject to penalties. This does not apply to persons who reported in good faith but whose information turned out to be incorrect. If bad faith is suspected, seek legal advice before taking any action, as the distinction requires careful factual assessment.
